nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered lagoons and seaward coral reefs at depths of about 1–20 m. Generally observed hovering over rockwork rather than perching on the substrate, retreating into small holes when threatened.

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a wide range of prepared foods including chopped crustaceans, mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, frozen zooplankton, and spirulina-based herbivore preparations. Tends to graze opportunistically on small crustaceans (copepods, amphipods) associated with live rock; established refugium-fed microfauna is generally regarded as a strong supplement.

summary: A striking black-and-yellow Western Pacific fang blenny. Generally considered reef-safe and is one of the more visible, hover-in-the-water-column blennies, making it a popular community-reef pick.
careNotes:
The Striped Blenny (Meiacanthus grammistes), also widely sold as the Striped Poison-Fang Blenny or Striped Fang Blenny, is a Western Pacific combtooth blenny ranging from Indochina to Papua New Guinea, north to the Ryukyu Islands, and south to the Great Barrier Reef. It is one of the most boldly patterned Meiacanthus species — alternating black-and-yellow longitudinal stripes that resolve into spots toward the caudal peduncle.

Like all Meiacanthus, it possesses a pair of enlarged grooved canine fangs in the lower jaw connected to a venom gland — used defensively against predators rather than offensively. Bites to aquarists are uncommon but have been reported as painful; hand-feeding is generally advised against. The bold warning coloration is mimicked by the unrelated blenny Petroscirtes breviceps, which lacks venom.

Generally regarded as reef-safe; no widely reported cases of coral or invertebrate nipping. Tends to spend much of its time hovering in mid-water near rockwork, which makes it noticeably more visible than perching blennies. Sources commonly describe it as relatively hardy and peaceful toward unrelated tankmates, while being intolerant of conspecifics and other Meiacanthus species outside of mated pairs in mid-sized tanks.

Sources disagreed on minimum tank size: LiveAquaria lists 30 US gal (~114 L); Reefapp lists 150 L (40 gal) and notes the species is an active hoverer benefiting from ample swimming space. Per the playbook's cautious-larger tank-sizing rule for active mid-water fish, 150 L is used as a nicely rounded minimum. Adult size figures also varied — LiveAquaria 5 in (~12.7 cm); FishBase and Reefapp 11 cm TL — averaged to a 9–12 cm range here.

This species is a known jumper and a tight-fitting lid is strongly advised. As with other fang blennies, it tolerates standard reef parameters well but does best in established tanks with mature rockwork that supports a microfauna population (copepods, amphipods) for between-feeding grazing.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing; eggs are demersal and adhesive, attached to substrate via a filamentous pedestal, with the male typically guarding the clutch. Larvae are planktonic and have been reported in shallow coastal waters. Captive breeding has been achieved commercially for related Meiacanthus species (notably M. atrodorsalis), and rearing protocols using live rotifers as first food generally apply; rearing M. grammistes specifically remains moderately challenging and is not routinely offered as captive-bred.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source of pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), kH (8–12), temperature (72–78°F = 22.2–25.6°C), temperament (semi-aggressive), reef-safe status, omnivore diet, venomous-fang note, and 30 gal minimum (~114 L). Lists max size as 5 in (~12.7 cm).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Blenniidae / Blenniiformes; Valenciennes, 1836), max size (11 cm TL), distribution (Western Pacific: Indochina to Papua New Guinea, Ryukyu Is. to NW Australia and GBR), depth range (1–20 m), wild temperature (26.4–29.3 °C), spawning biology (oviparous, demersal adhesive eggs on filamentous pedestal), and mimicry by Petroscirtes breviceps. IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Reefapp encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (150 L = 40 gal), max size (11 cm), reef-safe status (always safe), conspecific aggression notes, jumping/escape risk (known to jump from open tanks), and hover-in-water-column behavior. Used to justify the 150 L cautious minimum and the 'high' escapeRisk.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous, distinct pairing, demersal adhesive eggs on a filamentous pedestal; male typically guards. Larvae require live rotifers as first food. Captive breeding has been achieved for some Meiacanthus species; routine commercial production of M. grammistes is not currently widespread.
